paper_metadata.title: Denial and distrust: explaining the partisan climate gap
paper_metadata.empirical_scope_or_sample: Non-probability Prolific survey of self-identified Democrats/Republicans (n=1808), collected Mar 22–30, 2021.
exact_tax_terms_used: A carbon tax on energy companies and utilities that will raise the cost of using fossil fuels and incentivize use of renewable energy sources
paper_metadata.carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Respondents indicated support/oppose for an explicit carbon tax; Kitagawa–Blinder–Oaxaca decompositions estimate how partisan gaps in carbon-tax support are explained by trust in science and climate-science denial (plus smaller roles for trust in government).

determinants.definition: Trust in the scientific community on issues related to climate change (definitely/probably/not sure/probably not/definitely not).
determinants.mechanism: Greater trust in scientific expertise increases perceived credibility and legitimacy of climate claims, making a carbon tax more acceptable.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: A carbon tax on energy companies and utilities that will raise the cost of using fossil fuels and incentivize use of renewable energy sources.
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for the carbon tax policy (support vs oppose/not sure).
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Carbon tax decomposition: trust(science) coefficient −0.13*** (total gap explained for carbon tax: 54.57%).
determinants.notes: Decomposition coefficients reflect the explained component of the partisan support gap (sign reported in table; higher trust corresponds to higher support among Democrats).

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Climate-science denial / low acceptance of climate evidence
determinants.definition: Beliefs about whether climate change is occurring, combining yes/no and certainty; operationalized as climate-science denial in the decomposition.
determinants.mechanism: More denial reduces acceptance of climate risks and the need for mitigation, lowering support for carbon taxes aimed at reducing fossil-fuel use.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: A carbon tax on energy companies and utilities that will raise the cost of using fossil fuels and incentivize use of renewable energy sources.
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for the carbon tax policy (support vs oppose/not sure).
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Carbon tax decomposition: climate denial coefficient −0.12*** (total gap explained for carbon tax: 54.57%).
determinants.notes: Denial is higher among Republicans and aligns with lower carbon-tax support; table sign reflects the reported decomposition orientation.

determinants.definition: Trust in government on issues related to climate change (definitely/probably/not sure/probably not/definitely not).
determinants.mechanism: Higher trust in government increases confidence that climate policy will be implemented effectively and fairly, supporting carbon-tax acceptability.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: A carbon tax on energy companies and utilities that will raise the cost of using fossil fuels and incentivize use of renewable energy sources.
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for the carbon tax policy (support vs oppose/not sure).
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Carbon tax decomposition: trust(gov’t) coefficient −0.01** (smaller contribution than trust in science and climate denial).
determinants.notes: Magnitude is small; sign reflects decomposition table orientation, while substantive direction is toward higher support with greater trust.

exclusions_or_uncertainties.reason: For the carbon tax column in Table 3, the affective polarization coefficient is reported as 0.00 (no significance), so no clear acceptability direction was extracted.

Authors note these variables are excluded from Table 3 for interpretation and provide only general statements about minor explanatory power on some policies without identifying the carbon-tax column specifically.
